title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Philosophy of Minimalist Living
content.subtitle: Beyond decluttering: Analyzing the psychological shift toward intentional consumption.
content.text: The modern minimalist movement, often popularized through images of stark, white-walled apartments and capsule wardrobes, is frequently dismissed as a superficial aesthetic trend for the wealthy. However, a deeper look reveals that minimalism is less about the things we discard and more about the psychological 'margin' we create. [31] At its core, it is a philosophical response to the 'decision fatigue' that plagues an over-stimulated society, where the sheer volume of choices—from breakfast cereals to career paths—can lead to a state of cognitive paralysis. [/31] By deliberately limiting our options, we are not just saving space; we are conserving the mental energy required for more significant moral and creative pursuits. || The history of minimalism can be traced back to the Stoics and the ascetic traditions of the East, both of which emphasized that human happiness is inversely proportional to our attachment to external objects. [32] While ancient philosophers sought spiritual enlightenment, the modern minimalist is often seeking 'mental clarity'—a state of being where the clutter of the physical world no longer interrupts the flow of internal thought. [/32] This shift is particularly relevant in the age of the 'attention economy,' where our digital lives are as cluttered as our physical ones. A minimalist today is someone who realizes that an unread email inbox is just as burdensome as a messy garage. || One of the primary psychological benefits of this lifestyle is the breaking of the 'hedonic treadmill'—the tendency of humans to quickly return to a stable level of happiness despite major positive or negative events or life changes. When we buy a new gadget, the dopamine spike is temporary; we soon adjust to the new 'normal' and begin searching for the next purchase. [33] Minimalism interrupts this cycle by shifting the focus from 'acquisition' to 'appreciation,' encouraging individuals to find value in the utility and beauty of what they already possess rather than the novelty of what they lack. [/33] It is a radical departure from the consumerist narrative that links self-worth to net worth. || [34] However, the author notes that the movement has been criticized for its inherent 'virtue signaling.' [/34] For some, the act of owning very little becomes a new kind of status symbol, a way to demonstrate one's discipline and elevated taste. This 'performative minimalism' can be just as obsessive and anxiety-inducing as the consumerism it seeks to replace. When the goal becomes the *appearance* of simplicity rather than the *experience* of it, the movement loses its transformative power, becoming just another brand to be curated on social media. || This brings us to the concept of 'Essentialism'—the disciplined pursuit of less. It is not just about getting rid of clothes; it is about saying 'no' to social obligations, projects, and relationships that do not align with one's core values. [35] This requires a degree of social courage, as it often means rejecting the cultural expectation of 'busyness' as a marker of importance. [/35] To be an essentialist is to accept that you cannot be everything to everyone, and that by spreading yourself thin, you are making a millimeter of progress in a million directions rather than a mile of progress in one. || Ultimately, minimalism is a tool for reclaiming one’s life from the noise of the 21st century. [36] The writer suggests that the true measure of success in this endeavor is not the number of items left in one's home, but the quality of the attention one is able to pay to the world. [/36] It is an invitation to live a life that is 'wide' rather than just 'full.' By clearing the decks of the unnecessary, we create the space for the truly essential to emerge, transforming our relationship with both ourselves and the environment we inhabit.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, the author suggests that minimalism is primarily...
options: A) a trend focused on improving the aesthetic quality of our homes.
B) a strategy for managing the stress caused by having too many choices.
C) a way for wealthy individuals to justify their spending habits.
D) a reaction to the lack of career paths available in modern society.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [31], which mentions 'decision fatigue' and 'cognitive paralysis' caused by 'volume of choices.' A and C are views the author dismisses. D is a misreading of 'career paths' as a cause of stress, not a lack of paths.

questions.question: What is the main difference between ancient and modern minimalism according to the text?
options: A) Ancient philosophers were more concerned with physical clutter than modern ones.
B) Modern minimalism is entirely digital, while ancient minimalism was physical.
C) The ancient goal was spiritual, whereas the modern goal is psychological.
D) Modern minimalists have more difficulty achieving 'mental clarity' than the Stoics.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [32], stating ancient ones sought 'spiritual enlightenment' while modern ones seek 'mental clarity.' A is not stated. B is wrong as modern minimalism includes physical aspects. D is not a comparison made in the text.

questions.question: How does minimalism affect the 'hedonic treadmill' mentioned in the third paragraph?
options: A) It speeds up the treadmill by encouraging more frequent, smaller purchases.
B) It stops the treadmill by making individuals indifferent to happiness.
C) It replaces the need for dopamine with a sense of social obligation.
D) It changes the focus from getting new things to valuing current possessions.
questions.explanation: D is supported by [33] and the shift from 'acquisition' to 'appreciation.' A is the opposite of the point. B is too extreme. C is not mentioned.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the fourth paragraph?
options: A) To describe the aesthetic standards of minimalist social media influencers.
B) To warn that minimalism can become a new form of competitive consumption.
C) To argue that discipline is the most important part of a minimalist lifestyle.
D) To explain why wealthy people are more likely to become minimalists.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [34] and the mention of 'virtue signaling' and 'performative minimalism' as a 'status symbol.' A is a detail, not the purpose. C and D are not the main points of this paragraph.

questions.question: The writer suggests that becoming an 'Essentialist' requires 'social courage' because...
options: A) it is physically difficult to get rid of most of one's belongings.
B) it involves going against the popular idea that being busy means you are successful.
C) it requires a person to move to a new community with similar values.
D) it often leads to a complete loss of all social and professional relationships.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [35] and the mention of rejecting the 'cultural expectation of busyness.' A and C are not mentioned. D is an exaggeration ('complete loss').

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the author defines minimalist success as...
options: A) achieving a home that is completely free of all unnecessary objects.
B) being able to participate more effectively in the attention economy.
C) the ability to focus deeply on what is truly important in life.
D) finding a way to make progress in many different directions at once.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [36], stating the measure of success is the 'quality of attention' one pays to the world. A is explicitly rejected. B is the opposite of the author's intent. D is what an essentialist seeks to avoid.
